sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP action didn't seem to move the player - the checkerboard is still at the same position. Let me check more carefully what changed. The board_changed flag should tell me if anything changed. Let me also check if the player actually moved by comparing the before and after frames.
Wait, looking at the output, the player is still at rows 34-37, cols 9-12 (after the RIGHT move). The UP action didn't move it. Maybe the player can only move in certain directions, or maybe it's blocked.
Actually wait - let me re-read. After RIGHT, the player was at cols 9-12 (shifted from 8-11). After UP, the output shows the same pattern at rows 34-37. So the player didn't move up.
Let me check if the player is blocked by the colored blocks above. The red/dark red blocks are at rows 30-33, and the player is at rows 34-37. So there's no blocking between them...
Wait, let me re-examine. Maybe UP didn't work because the player is already at the boundary or something. Let me check the result more carefully and try other directions.
Actually, I should check if the player moved at all. Let me compare the before and after frames properly.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if UP actually moved the player - compare before/after
if last_transition:
    before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    after_ascii = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    
    # Find differences
    diffs = []
    for i in range(len(before_ascii)):
        if before_ascii[i] != after_ascii[i]:
            diffs.append(i)
    print("Rows changed:", diffs)
    if diffs:
        for r in diffs:
            print(f"  Row {r}:")
            print(f"    before: {before_ascii[r][6:16]}")
            print(f"    after:  {after_ascii[r][6:16]}")
else:
    print("No transition to check")

print("\nLast action result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The UP action didn't move the checkerboard player. Instead, it changed the gray square with the green inside (rows 38-43). The green 2x2 inside moved from rows 40-41 to rows 38-39. So UP moved the green inner piece inside the gray square, not the player!
This means the arrow keys don't move the player directly. They might control something else - perhaps they move the inner colored pieces inside the gray squares, or they control which object is "selected" and then move that object.
Let me reconsider: maybe the checkerboard pattern ISN'T the player. Maybe it's just another object on the board. The arrow keys might be controlling the inner colored pieces (yellow, green, etc.) inside the gray containers.
Let me test this theory:
- The green piece moved UP inside its gray container
- Let me try RIGHT to see if the green piece moves right
- Or maybe the arrow keys cycle through which object is selected, then move it
Actually, looking more carefully:
- The green piece was at rows 40-41, cols 10-11 (inside the gray square at rows 38-43, cols 8-13)
- After UP, it moved to rows 38-39, cols 10-11
- So it moved up by 2 rows inside its container
Let me test if RIGHT moves the green piece right, or if it moves to a different container.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The UP moved the green piece inside its gray container, not the checkerboard
# Let me test RIGHT to see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT'])

# Check what changed
if last_transition:
    before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    after_ascii = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    
    diffs = []
    for i in range(len(before_ascii)):
        if before_ascii[i] != after_ascii[i]:
            diffs.append(i)
    print("Rows changed:", diffs)
    if diffs:
        for r in diffs:
            print(f"  Row {r}:")
            print(f"    before: {before_ascii[r][6:16]}")
            print(f"    after:  {after_ascii[r][6:16]}")

# Also check the green piece area
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Green piece area (rows 36-44, cols 6-16) ===")
for i in range(36, 44):
    print(f"  {i}: {ascii_lines[i][6:16]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
